text: The patient is a sixty-two year old female with stage one sigmoid colon cancer. We show here a video of laparoscopic sigmoidectomy by needle scopic surgery.

The surgeon places a 12mm port at the umbilicus for a 10mm scope, followed by a 5mm port at the right lower quadrant. Additionally, three 3.5mm ports are positioned at the right and left lateral and left lower quadrants.

India ink is visible near the tumor site. The surgeon begins medial retroperitoneal dissection of the mesocolon using an electric scalpel inserted through the right lower five millimeter port. The other three ports are equipped with three point five millimeter forceps, and the dissection progresses cranially.

The surgeon exposes and cuts the root of the inferior mesenteric artery using an ultrasonic device after clipping it. Retroperitoneal dissection continues laterally, with the left ureter and gonadal vessels identified and preserved.

The surgeon clips and cuts the inferior mesenteric vein and left colic artery. The lateral attachment is separated by dissecting the taut fusion fascia, progressing towards the median dissection plane.
The surgeon dissects the retrolectal space, carefully identifying and preserving the bilateral hypogastric nerves during the laparoscopic sigmoidectomy procedure.

The surgeon dissects the retroperitoneum on both the right and left sides of the upper rectum. Subsequently, the mesocolon is dissected, revealing the colonic walls along the cut line of the distal cone.

A five-millimeter scope is inserted through the right lower port, providing a view for the procedure. A forceps, intestinal clip, and articulated-type linear stapler are inserted through the twelve-millimeter port for colonic transduction. An intestinal clip is placed at the oral side of the colonic cut line, with the angle adjusted using an angle adjuster. The angle of the N-linear stapler is also adjusted.
The specimen is extracted through a small skin incision at the umbilicus, measuring up to thirty-five millimeters. The surgeon performs an anastomosis using a double stapling technique.
The surgeon places a nine-inch French drain into the pelvis through the right lower five-millimeter incision. The small wounds from the procedure are barely visible twenty-five days postoperatively.
